state.prose:
As of 2026-03-06, recent behavioral observations (overall confidence: high):

Sleep:
  • Sleep onset has been around 00:15 (about 1.0h later than typical 23:16); (yesterday only) [moderate, coverage: high]
  • Sleep has averaged 7.0h (0.6h shorter than typical 7.6h); (yesterday only) [mild, coverage: high]

Screen & attention:
  • Late-night screen use up 100% vs personal baseline (64min vs typical 32min); (yesterday only) [moderate, coverage: high]
  • Attention pattern more fragmented than baseline (1.5 vs 1.2 switches/active-min); (yesterday only) [mild, coverage: high]

Coherent patterns worth noting:
  • Sleep onset is drifting later alongside increased late-night screen use — a phone-mediated sleep-delay pattern. Evening calendar density and wind-down timing are plausible leverage points.
    (signals: sleep_onset_hour, late_night_screen_min)
  • Attention is more fragmented while sleep is shorter — attention costs typically rise when sleep debt accumulates. Worth protecting sleep opportunity over discretionary evening commitments.
    (signals: app_switching_rate, sleep_duration_hours)

The LLM scheduler should decide which of the above are worth acting on through calendar adjustments, based on the user's preferences, upcoming commitments, and the coherence of the pattern — not on single-day magnitudes.

coherent_patterns.interpretation: Sleep onset shifted ~1 hour later (to ~00:15) alongside a doubling of late-night screen use (64 min vs. 32 min baseline). These two markers cohere as a phone-mediated sleep-delay pattern: screen engagement in the late evening is plausibly delaying the transition to sleep. The prior hypothesis from deterministic rules is confirmed and the acute-1d trajectory makes it timely to act on.
coherent_patterns.literature_basis: Exelmans & Van den Bulck — late-night screen use and sleep onset delay
coherent_patterns.name: phone-mediated-sleep-delay

coherent_patterns.interpretation: App-switching rate is mildly elevated (1.47 vs. 1.20 switches/active-min) on the same day that sleep was ~0.6 h shorter than usual. These co-occur in a pattern where reduced sleep opportunity tends to raise attentional switching costs during waking hours. Prior hypothesis confirmed; both markers remain acute (1-day trajectory).
coherent_patterns.literature_basis: Fragmented attention with sleep loss — sleep-debt cognitive strain
coherent_patterns.name: fragmented-attention-with-sleep-loss

response.salience_reasoning: All four deviations are acute (yesterday only) and high-coverage, so they are fresh and actionable rather than entrenched. The two confirmed coherent patterns share a common upstream lever: if late-night screen use is curtailed, sleep onset can shift earlier, sleep duration can recover, and attentional fragmentation the following day is likely to ease. The most impactful single calendar move is therefore a wind-down block before the current late-evening screen window. A secondary lever is the 'optional work session' tonight (21:00–22:30), which is marked flexible — shortening or capping it reduces the risk of another late screen session running past midnight. Given the user's preference for low-burden changes and sleep protection, two targeted adjustments are sufficient; no need to restructure the full week on a one-day signal.

suggestions.change: Add a 'Wind-down / screens off' block at 22:30–23:00 each evening this week
suggestions.rationale: Late-night screen use doubled last night and sleep onset shifted ~1 hour later. A short, recurring wind-down block creates a visible cue to close apps and begin transitioning to sleep, targeting the phone-mediated-sleep-delay pattern at its earliest leverage point.

suggestions.change: Shorten tonight's 'optional work session' to end by 22:00 (from 22:30)
suggestions.rationale: The session is marked flexible and currently ends at 22:30, leaving little buffer before the recommended wind-down. Trimming 30 minutes reduces the chance of screen activity spilling past 22:30 and repeating last night's delayed onset. This is a minimal change that respects the user's low-burden preference.

state.prose:
As of 2026-03-07, recent behavioral observations (overall confidence: high):

Sleep:
  • Sleep onset has been around 01:07 (about 1.9h later than typical 23:16); sustained across the last 2d [pronounced, coverage: high]
  • Sleep has averaged 6.3h (1.3h shorter than typical 7.5h); sustained across the last 2d [pronounced, coverage: high]
  • Sleep schedule has been less consistent (SRI 74 vs typical 82); sustained across the last 2d [mild, coverage: high]

Screen & attention:
  • Late-night screen use up 229% vs personal baseline (100min vs typical 30min); sustained across the last 2d [pronounced, coverage: high]
  • Total screen time elevated (343min/day vs typical 277min, +23%); sustained across the last 2d [moderate, coverage: high]
  • Attention pattern more fragmented than baseline (1.9 vs 1.2 switches/active-min); sustained across the last 2d [pronounced, coverage: high]

Coherent patterns worth noting:
  • Sleep onset is drifting later alongside increased late-night screen use — a phone-mediated sleep-delay pattern. Evening calendar density and wind-down timing are plausible leverage points.
    (signals: sleep_onset_hour, late_night_screen_min)
  • Sleep schedule consistency has dropped; circadian rhythm is less stable than typical. Anchoring morning wake time and fixed daily events can help.
    (signals: sleep_regularity_index, sleep_onset_hour)
  • Attention is more fragmented while sleep is shorter — attention costs typically rise when sleep debt accumulates. Worth protecting sleep opportunity over discretionary evening commitments.
    (signals: app_switching_rate, sleep_duration_hours)

The LLM scheduler should decide which of the above are worth acting on through calendar adjustments, based on the user's preferences, upcoming commitments, and the coherence of the pattern — not on single-day magnitudes.

coherent_patterns.interpretation: Sleep onset has shifted ~1.9h later than baseline (to ~01:07), co-occurring with a 229% spike in late-night screen use (100 min vs. typical 30 min). The screen activity is the most plausible proximate driver of the delayed onset, consistent with the phone-mediated sleep-delay mechanism. Both markers are sustained over 2 days, strengthening the signal.
coherent_patterns.literature_basis: Exelmans & Van den Bulck — late-night phone use and sleep onset delay
coherent_patterns.name: phone-mediated-sleep-delay

coherent_patterns.interpretation: Sleep regularity index has dropped from ~82 to ~74 alongside the later and more variable sleep onset. The combination indicates the sleep schedule is losing its anchor, not just shifting uniformly. Fixing a consistent wake time is a standard leverage point.
coherent_patterns.name: circadian-instability

coherent_patterns.interpretation: App-switching rate is up 66% above baseline while sleep duration is down ~1.3h/night. Elevated screen time overall (343 vs. 277 min/day) adds context: more time on device, less focused engagement. Short sleep and high switching rate are co-occurring in a way consistent with sleep-debt cognitive strain.
coherent_patterns.literature_basis: Fragmented attention with sleep loss — sleep-debt cognitive strain pattern
coherent_patterns.name: fragmented-attention-with-sleep-loss

questions_for_user: Is the late-night screen use tied to a specific activity (e.g., studying, streaming, social media)? Knowing this would help tailor the wind-down suggestion more precisely.
response.salience_reasoning: All six deviations are high-coverage and sustained over 2 days, so confidence in the signal is high. The three coherent patterns all converge on the same root bottleneck: late-night screen use is delaying sleep onset, compressing sleep duration, destabilizing the circadian schedule, and likely contributing to fragmented daytime attention. The most actionable calendar lever is the 'optional work session' tonight (21:00–22:30), which is both flexible and sits squarely in the window where screen use is spiking. Shifting or capping that block, and adding a wind-down buffer before a target sleep time, addresses all three patterns simultaneously. A consistent morning anchor event would reinforce circadian stability. The total_screen_min elevation is largely explained by the late-night spike and does not independently warrant a separate calendar action.

suggestions.change: End or reschedule the 'optional work session' by 21:30 at the latest, freeing a wind-down buffer before a 23:00 target sleep window
suggestions.rationale: The optional work session runs until 22:30, directly overlapping the window where late-night screen use has spiked to 100 min/night. Capping it 30–60 min earlier creates space to reduce screen exposure before sleep and nudge onset back toward baseline. The event is marked flexible, so this is a low-disruption change.

suggestions.change: Add a 'Wind-down block' at 22:00–23:00 (no work apps, low-stimulation activity)
suggestions.rationale: A protected pre-sleep buffer directly counters the late-night screen pattern and gives the circadian system a consistent nightly cue. Pairing it with the earlier work-session cutoff creates a two-step ramp rather than an abrupt stop.

suggestions.change: Add a fixed 'Morning anchor' block at 07:30–08:00 each weekday (e.g., a brief walk or consistent breakfast routine)
suggestions.rationale: Sleep regularity index has dropped alongside the shifting onset. A consistent, low-effort morning anchor helps stabilize the circadian rhythm from the wake side, complementing the evening wind-down. The existing 08:30 class on Saturday already provides one anchor; extending this pattern to other days reinforces it.

coherent_patterns.interpretation: Sleep onset is running ~1.4h later than usual (around 01:07), co-occurring with late-night screen use that has nearly doubled to ~98 min. coherent_patterns.name: phone-mediated-sleep-delay

coherent_patterns.interpretation: Sleep regularity index has dropped from ~80 to ~73, alongside the shifted onset. Irregular sleep timing compounds the sleep-debt effect and makes recovery harder. Anchoring a consistent wind-down time is the primary lever.

coherent_patterns.interpretation: App-switching rate is up ~40% above baseline while sleep duration is down ~1.1h. Elevated total screen time (+27%) suggests the extra screen hours are distributed across many short sessions rather than focused use, consistent with sleep-debt-related attention strain.
coherent_patterns.literature_basis: Saeb et al. / general sleep-debt cognitive-load literature

questions_for_user: The 'optional work session' tonight (21:00–22:30) is marked flexible — is there a deadline driving it, or is it discretionary? That would help calibrate how much it can be trimmed.
response.salience_reasoning: Three overlapping patterns all converge on the same root mechanism: late-night screen use is pushing sleep onset later, shortening sleep, and destabilizing the sleep schedule. The fragmented-attention signal is likely a downstream consequence rather than an independent cause. The comm_reciprocity dip is a single-day, medium-coverage observation and does not yet form a coherent pattern — it warrants a light watch but not a calendar action. The most impactful calendar lever is the 'optional work session' tonight (21:00–22:30), which sits squarely in the window where late-night screen use is accumulating. Replacing or capping it with a wind-down block would directly address all three patterns simultaneously. A consistent morning anchor would reinforce circadian stability. These two changes are low-burden and align with the user's stated preference to protect sleep.

suggestions.change: Replace or cap the 'optional work session' (21:00–22:30) with a Wind-down block starting at 21:30, ending at 22:30 — treat the first 30 min as the last work window and the final 60 min as screen-off wind-down.
suggestions.rationale: Late-night screen use has nearly doubled and is the most direct driver of the delayed sleep onset and shortened sleep. The optional work session ends at 22:30, which leaves little buffer before a healthy sleep time. Capping active screen work at 21:30 and protecting 22:30 as a wind-down boundary creates a realistic path to an earlier sleep onset without cancelling the session entirely.

suggestions.change: Add a fixed 'Morning anchor' block at 07:30–08:00 each weekday — a brief, consistent wake-time routine (e.g., no-phone first 15 min, light exposure).
suggestions.rationale: Sleep regularity index has dropped, meaning the sleep schedule is less consistent day-to-day. A fixed morning anchor is one of the most evidence-supported ways to stabilize circadian timing without requiring an earlier bedtime on the first night.

suggestions.change: Add a 'Focus block — no app switching' for 90 min during the existing flexible study block (15:00–16:30), with notifications silenced.
suggestions.rationale: App-switching rate is elevated alongside shortened sleep, which tends to make sustained focus harder. Carving out a protected, single-task window during an already-scheduled flexible study block costs nothing on the calendar and may partially offset the attention fragmentation without requiring extra time.

Mobility & location routine:
  • Location routine more restricted than baseline (entropy 1.30 vs 1.84); (yesterday only) [pronounced, coverage: high]
  • More time at top-3 frequent places (75% vs typical 59%); (yesterday only) [moderate, coverage: high]

Social rhythm & communication:
  • Daily routine timing less regular (SRM 0.70 vs typical 0.81); (yesterday only) [mild, coverage: high]

Coherent patterns worth noting:

coherent_patterns.interpretation: All three mobility and social-rhythm markers moved together yesterday: location entropy dropped sharply, time at familiar places rose, and daily routine timing became less regular. This is consistent with a day of staying close to home and departing from the usual activity schedule — a pattern that matches behavioral-withdrawal signatures in the literature. The acute-1d trajectory means it is a single-day observation, so the pattern is real but not yet sustained.
coherent_patterns.literature_basis: Saeb et al. mobility-entropy depression correlate; Monk et al. Social Rhythm Metric irregularity
coherent_patterns.name: behavioral-withdrawal

questions_for_user: Yesterday's routine looked more home-bound than usual — was that intentional (e.g., a rest day or deadline focus day), or did something disrupt your normal schedule? This would help distinguish a deliberate choice from an unplanned pattern.
response.salience_reasoning: All three deviations are acute (yesterday only) and co-occur in a single coherent pattern rather than being independent signals, which raises their collective salience. However, the trajectory is acute-1d — one day does not establish a trend. The user's calendar today (Friday) already has a late flexible work block running until 22:00, which could compound routine irregularity if it runs long or displaces a wind-down. The most actionable lever is protecting schedule regularity and ensuring the evening work block has a clear end, so the day's anchor events don't drift further. No sleep-specific deviations are present, so sleep-protection nudges are secondary. Given the user's preference for low-burden changes, one light structural nudge and one rhythm-anchoring suggestion are appropriate; padding with more would not be warranted for a single-day pattern.

suggestions.change: Add a short 'Close-out & wrap' block at 22:00 to mark a firm end to the deadline work block and signal transition to wind-down
suggestions.rationale: The deadline work block ends at 22:00 but flexible blocks often overrun. A brief explicit close-out event creates a routine anchor at a consistent time, counteracting the mild social-rhythm irregularity observed yesterday and preventing further drift in daily timing.

suggestions.change: Add a brief 'Get outside / change of scene' reminder block tomorrow morning (Saturday)
suggestions.rationale: Yesterday's location entropy was pronounced — movement was notably more restricted than usual. A low-commitment prompt to step outside or visit a different location tomorrow can help restore typical mobility variety without requiring a large schedule change, consistent with the user's preference for low-burden adjustments.
